name: Alabama
note: Alabama bail bondsmen are now licensed by the state's dedicated Professional Bail Bonding Board rather than the Department of Insurance.

name: Arkansas
note: Arkansas sets the bail bond premium by statute at exactly 10% of the bond, with a $50 minimum.
regulator: Arkansas Professional Bail Bondsman Licensing Board (Department of Labor and Licensing)

name: Arizona
regulator: Arizona Department of Insurance and Financial Institutions

name: California
note: California caps bail premiums at 10%, and many agents offer filed discount rates of 7-8% for clients with an attorney or union membership.

name: Connecticut
note: Connecticut bail premiums are fixed by state law on a sliding scale — 10% of the first $5,000 of bond and 7% of everything above — and agents may not discount them.

name: District of Columbia
note: Washington, DC effectively eliminated money bail in 1992 — more than 90% of defendants are released on non-financial conditions through the Pretrial Services Agency, and commercial bondsmen do not operate.

name: Florida
note: Florida sets the bail premium at a flat 10% for state charges and 15% for federal bonds, uniform across all licensed agents.

name: Georgia
note: Georgia caps bail premiums at 15% and requires bondsmen to be approved by the sheriff of each county where they write bonds.
regulator: County sheriffs (bonding approval); surety agents licensed by the Georgia Office of the Commissioner of Insurance and Safety Fire

name: Hawaii
regulator: Hawaii Insurance Division (Department of Commerce and Consumer Affairs)

name: Illinois
note: Illinois abolished cash bail entirely in September 2023 under the Pretrial Fairness Act — release is determined by a judge without money bond.

name: Kentucky
note: Kentucky became the first state to ban commercial bail bonding in 1976 — defendants post bail deposits directly with the court.

name: Louisiana
note: Louisiana's bail premium is set by the legislature at 12% of the bond (minimum $120), higher than the 10% common elsewhere.

name: Massachusetts
note: Massachusetts has no commercial bail bond industry — cash bail is paid directly to the court or a bail commissioner rather than through a bondsman.

name: Maryland
note: A 2017 Maryland court rule directs judges to prefer non-financial release conditions, which has reduced — but not eliminated — the use of cash bail and bondsmen.

name: Maine
note: Maine has no commercial bail bond industry — release is handled by bail commissioners, and any cash bail is paid directly to the court.

name: Michigan
regulator: Michigan Department of Insurance and Financial Services

name: Missouri
regulator: Missouri Department of Commerce and Insurance

name: Montana
regulator: Montana Commissioner of Securities and Insurance

name: North Carolina
note: North Carolina law caps bail bond premiums at 15% of the bond amount, enforced statewide by the Department of Insurance.

name: Nebraska
note: Nebraska prohibits commercial bail bondsmen — defendants post a 10% cash deposit directly with the court instead.

name: New Jersey
note: New Jersey's 2017 Criminal Justice Reform Act replaced cash bail with risk-based release, effectively eliminating the commercial bail industry in the state.

name: New Mexico
note: New Mexico's 2016 constitutional amendment largely replaced money bail with risk-based release and detention, leaving only a small remaining bail bond market.
regulator: New Mexico Office of Superintendent of Insurance

name: Nevada
note: Nevada law sets the bail premium at exactly 15% of the bond (or $50, whichever is greater) — agents cannot legally discount it.

name: New York
note: New York's 2020 bail reform eliminated cash bail for most misdemeanors and nonviolent felonies, so licensed bondsmen operate only in the narrower set of bail-eligible cases.
regulator: New York State Department of Financial Services

name: Oregon
note: Oregon abolished commercial bail in 1974 — defendants post a 10% security deposit directly with the court instead of using a bondsman.

name: Pennsylvania
note: Philadelphia courts release most defendants on a 10% deposit paid directly to the court, so commercial bondsmen operate mainly elsewhere in Pennsylvania.

name: Rhode Island
regulator: Rhode Island Department of Business Regulation (Insurance Division)

name: Tennessee
note: As of 2026 Tennessee bondsmen must hold a license from the new state Board of Professional Bondsmen in addition to approval by the local courts where they operate.
regulator: Tennessee Board of Professional Bondsmen (Department of Commerce & Insurance), plus local court approval

name: Texas
note: Texas regulates bail bonds county-by-county through local bail bond boards, so licensing rules and typical rates can vary between counties.
regulator: County bail bond boards (Texas Occupations Code ch. 1704)

name: Virginia
note: Virginia is unusual in licensing bondsmen through its Department of Criminal Justice Services, with premiums fixed by law between 10% and 15%.
regulator: Virginia Department of Criminal Justice Services

name: Wisconsin
note: Wisconsin abolished commercial bail bonding in 1979 — bail is posted in cash directly with the court, though a friend or family member may act as surety.

name: West Virginia
regulator: West Virginia Offices of the Insurance Commissioner
